item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder how this SCP would impact SCP-499.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a thing what kills you with little narrative holding it up. Adding more of the backstory Aelanna suggested would help, or the crosstest with When Day Breaks. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, now that I read ChaoSarah's post, I really want to see a cross-test between this and SCP-2022. Someone make that happen!

EDIT: You know what, after ten seconds of thinking, I realized if this artifact works the way I think and as efficiently as it should, then 2022 (being literal sunlight) wouldn't do anything. In fact, someone effected by 1008 might not even be able to see instances of 2022.

I just made myself upvote this! Lol!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, could this be the ultimate way to avoid SCP-001 When Day Breaks? I mean, yeah, you'd die, but it's better than being melted into a conglomerate with several other compromised organisms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The core idea is pretty cool, honestly, if fairly straightforward(A lot of cultures, real and fictional, worship the Sun). Couple issues with it though, that bring it into definite downvote territory.

which lasts approximately two (2) to three (3) weeks after stage one
typically four (4) to six (6) weeks after initial exposure

Consistency please? This makes it incredibly unclear what the timeline is like because you're not using the same reference points between stages. It LOOKS like…
Week 1: Stage One
Weeks 2-4: Stage Two
Weeks 4-6: Stage Three?
But this doesn't account for the potential lack of overlap for a 2 week duration Stage Two, which leaves anywhere from 1-3 weeks of…nothing? Kind of a problem.

Addendum 1008-01: Transcript Excerpt of Interview with Subject 1008-01

Just this entire thing. When is this happening? Is this right before he goes on life support? How is he so INSANELY calm, given that when this starts he has NO IDEA what's happening? How does he do LITERALLY ANYTHING without the ability to SEE, since you need artificial light to see anything? And the last line from the agent just makes no sense. Does the stone give him some kind of prescience about the effect it's having on him? How does he have the connection between the sun giving life to things and no longer being able to eat? For that matter, why would it be stopping him from eating? The notes only say it prevents the subject from getting nutrition from them, not eating in the first place.

The first one is just a quick syntax fix, but that addendum kills the entire thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Created a unique and interesting way to die. +1 for that. But, I do feel like this SCP leaves absolutely nothing to the imagination, which can be a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well everybody else eats solar-powered products like plants and animals which eat those plants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if you expose somebody to this and then give them a pill of SCP-2022

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, quite the devastating punishment. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing containing the subject and all testing equipment would malfunction before the sun would have its effect (read "no effect") on the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna see them launch someone into space at the sun and see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if you could use nuclear power to provide radiant heat/multispectrum light/grow edible plants, and preserve a subject's life and relative wellbeing that way?

What really makes this a head-scratcher for me is that bit about everything else looking malnourished and stunted to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's similar to what I was thinking though I was thinking along the lines of artificial methods. For instance would plants grown under UV lights work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously not what you were thinking, but I got the image of someone spontaneously devolving into a sludge of chemosynthetic bacteria… which is hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From 2011; disregard if you use the activity feed to check new entries.

I'd say Aelanna's summary is spot-on, but as times have changed, I think this should be an almost fatal curse. We now know that life, as advanced as the phylum Chordata even, exists in the deep sea, a place where sunlight is completely blotted out. And warmth can be derived through natural hot springs/the earth's mantle. Could someone with this curse not survive through the means of very special containment procedures? Completely changing someone's way of life to exclude everything derived from the sun would have interesting psychological effects no doubt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very very belated clarification, as looking back on some of the comments in this thread, there may be some confusion as to how this works or the religious implications behind it.

Imagine that you were a member of an ancient tribe of sun-worshippers. As punishment for a serious crime against the tribe, the shaman would curse you to never draw warmth from the sun (their god) again, effectively exiling you from the source of all life. Over time, all that lives by the sun's power shuns you, until you die cold and alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sarcasm doesn't seem to work on the internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not really a worthwhile test. The Foundation honestly probably wouldn't bother testing this on increasingly saintly people, simply because that knowledge doesn't really get you anything, and abducting and killing random innocents (don't get too many of those in the D-class population), is nastier than the Foundation would do without a real good reason.

Besides, it probably doesn't even work that way. An electric chair will kill anyone you use it on, regardless of guilt or innocence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
common sacrificing innocent people the foundation isn't like that

Well of course not. I daresay our sacrifices of innocent people are by uncommon methods, methods for the discerning scholar of the unknown. Not some random jibber-jabber human sacrifice. we have class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, you're responding to a post that's nearly 11 months old, not the worst necro I've seen but pretty long.
Secondly, it's "come on", "common" means "not rare".

Thirdly:

sacrificing innocent people the foundation isn't like that.

Hi, you must be new, welcome to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like that too maybe an addendum, just to test it out but i don't think the foundation would do that. I mean common sacrificing innocent people the foundation isn't like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just upvoted this to +70. This brings to mind several biblical prophecies concerning the sun and moon being blotted out, but made uniquely creepy in that it is limited to a single individual's own perception and body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they couldn't see stars, but could see the sun, I'd agree with you. But the sun is a specific star, and much like demolishing the statue of liberty doesn't destroy all landmarks, there's no reason why removing the sun from someone's reality must remove all the stars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey OP, I'd like to request permission to attempt a picture. Since I only speak modern English and Spanish, can I use Dr Afterdawn's translation for the words? Let me know what you think, and in the meantime, I'll start making the picture. (If you'd like a sample of my work, I have two other pictures on the site, 294 and 1243.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this kind of creepy myself.

I do have one issue with it, regarding the fact that they can't see the sun, which is a star, but they can see other stars.

But if I suspend my disbelief and just go, "Well, I guess the ancient people who made this didn't know the sun was a star and this lack of knowledge made it specific to the sun," then I guess it's okay. It still bugs me for some reason, but, hey, it's fiction, and the concept is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if anything would happen if you put this on an "innocent" person?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also my signature.

Really, really coincidental.

… this is kinda spooky now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually Indonesian, and a possible back-translation of the curse at the end would be:

Pendosa terhukum
terusir
Oleh Sang Hyang Cahya
Terbinasa dalam Gelap.

It's not quite ancient Indonesian, but it uses some uncommon, ancient words.

Another strangeness: my nickname is somehow very fitting for this SCP. Coincidental, I swear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Forgettable?!

…for the record, I thought it was creepy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's kinda… forgettable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh. Not bad.
